personality_voice.book_baseline: Book Sirius is defined by fierce loyalty, barely-contained rage, and a dark wit sharpened by twelve years of wrongful imprisonment. His voice is raw and urgent - he speaks in short, pressured bursts when emotional ('THEN YOU SHOULD HAVE DIED!'), but can be coldly precise when confronting Pettigrew ('I'll tell you why... Because you never did anything for anyone unless you could see what was in it for you'). He is impulsive to the point of recklessness - Lupin physically has to restrain him from killing Pettigrew on the spot. He shows genuine tenderness toward Harry, but it is awkward and tentative, the tenderness of a man who has forgotten how to be gentle. He is also capable of petty cruelty - he makes no effort to stop Snape's head from bumping the tunnel ceiling. His dominant emotional register is barely-suppressed violence alternating with sudden, startling warmth.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core of Sirius's intensity and loyalty. His line 'I did my waiting! Twelve years of it! In Azkaban! Trust me, you wouldn't have lasted a week!' captures the compressed rage of the books. His confrontation with Pettigrew ('Died! Died rather than betray my friends!') is faithful in content. However, the film adds a sentimental, almost sage-like register that is absent from the books. His farewell speech - 'Trust yourself... you'll be surprised how many times you can find the answers... here' (tapping his heart) - and 'A shooting star. Make a wish' are invented and tonally inconsistent with the book character, who is too damaged and impulsive to deliver composed wisdom. The film also removes his petty vindictiveness toward Snape and his cold, skull-like fury during the Pettigrew confrontation.
personality_voice.difference: The film adds a composed, warm mentor register that the books do not support. Book Sirius is not a sage; he is a traumatized man running on obsession and loyalty. The invented farewell dialogue replaces the book's awkward, tentative godfather offer ('I'll understand, of course, if you want to stay with your aunt and uncle') with polished emotional closure. The film also removes his impulsive violence - in the books he lunges at Pettigrew and has to be physically restrained multiple times; in the film he is more controlled. His dark wit toward Snape ('Piss off') survives but is reduced.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The addition of an invented mentor-sage register is a meaningful personality change, not mere compression. The book's Sirius is defined partly by what he cannot do - he cannot be calm, cannot be composed, cannot deliver wisdom without it being undercut by his rage or his awkwardness. The film's farewell scene invents a version of Sirius that the books explicitly do not contain. This warrants a penalty in the 14-17 range: the character remains recognizable, but an important tonal register is changed and a false one added.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Sirius's narrative function in PoA is threefold: (1) he is the apparent threat that drives the plot's tension for most of the book; (2) he is the agent who exposes Pettigrew - he escapes Azkaban specifically to find and kill Pettigrew, and it is his knowledge, his plan, and his confrontation that forces the truth into the open; (3) he is the emotional resolution for Harry - the godfather who offers a home. His agency is high: he breaks out of Azkaban through his own ingenuity, he communicates with Crookshanks to steal passwords, he corners Pettigrew, he explains the full history of the Marauders, and he offers Harry a future. He also gives Harry the Firebolt and writes the letter granting Hogsmeade permission.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The film preserves Sirius's role as the apparent threat and as the agent who exposes Pettigrew. His confrontation with Pettigrew is intact in structure. However, several specific acts of agency are removed or transferred: the Firebolt gift and the Hogsmeade permission letter (which in the books are the final proof of his care for Harry and his practical role as godfather) are absent from the film corpus. The explanation of how he escaped Azkaban - a key moment of agency and self-revelation in the books - is absent from the film corpus. The Crookshanks collaboration (Sirius communicating with the cat to steal passwords) is absent. In the Shrieking Shack, Lupin does more of the explaining than in the books, where Sirius and Lupin share the exposition more equally.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Firebolt subplot, the Hogsmeade letter, the Azkaban escape explanation, and the Crookshanks collaboration are all absent from the film. These are not decorative - the Firebolt and letter demonstrate Sirius's active, practical care for Harry from a distance; the escape explanation is a major moment of self-disclosure and agency; the Crookshanks collaboration shows Sirius's intelligence and resourcefulness. Lupin takes a larger share of the exposition in the film's Shrieking Shack scene.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The loss of the Firebolt, the Hogsmeade letter, and the escape explanation removes concrete demonstrations of Sirius's agency and his practical role as godfather. These are not redundant - they are the only evidence in the book that Sirius has been actively caring for Harry throughout the year, not just pursuing Pettigrew. Their absence weakens his narrative function as godfather. The transfer of some exposition to Lupin is a minor agency reduction. The core function - exposing Pettigrew - is preserved. Score in the 14-17 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Sirius's motivations are layered: (1) he wants to kill Pettigrew as an act of justice and self-vindication; (2) he is driven by guilt - he persuaded James and Lily to switch Secret-Keepers to Pettigrew, and he has spent twelve years knowing he is 'as good as' responsible for their deaths; (3) he wants to protect Harry, the last living connection to James; (4) he wants his freedom and his name cleared. His internal conflict is between his murderous rage toward Pettigrew and his love for Harry - Harry's intervention is what stops him. His guilt is explicitly stated: 'I as good as killed them... I persuaded Lily and James to change to Peter at the last moment... I'm to blame, I know it.'
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the core motivation - killing Pettigrew for betraying James and Lily - and the desire to protect Harry. The line 'Died! Died rather than betray my friends!' captures the moral core. However, the guilt motivation - Sirius's knowledge that he suggested switching to Pettigrew and therefore bears responsibility - is entirely absent from the film corpus. This is not a minor detail; it is the source of his deepest internal conflict and explains why he says 'I as good as killed them' rather than simply 'Pettigrew killed them.'
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film removes Sirius's guilt about suggesting the switch to Pettigrew. In the books, this guilt is what makes his character tragic rather than merely wronged. He is not simply an innocent man; he is a man who made a catastrophic error of judgment and has lived with it for twelve years. The film presents him as purely wronged, which simplifies his internal conflict significantly.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The guilt motivation is not peripheral - it is the source of Sirius's self-description as 'as good as' a killer, and it complicates his moral status in ways the film does not replicate. Its absence flattens his internal conflict from 'wronged man who also bears partial responsibility' to 'wronged man.' This is a meaningful simplification, warranting a score in the 11-13 range rather than 14-15.

character_arc.book_baseline: Sirius's arc in PoA moves from: (1) feared fugitive/apparent villain; (2) revealed innocent, but still consumed by murderous rage toward Pettigrew; (3) restrained by Harry's moral intervention; (4) briefly free and offering Harry a home; (5) recaptured and then rescued; (6) forced back into hiding, the home he offered Harry impossible. The arc ends in loss - Harry's hope of living with Sirius is destroyed. Crucially, Sirius does not complete a redemptive transformation; he ends the book still a fugitive, still guilty about James and Lily, still unable to give Harry what he promised. The arc's emotional core is the brief, destroyed possibility of family.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows the same structural arc: fugitive to revealed innocent to restrained to briefly free to rescued to back in hiding. The godfather offer is present. However, the film's ending is tonally different: Sirius's farewell is composed and warm ('Trust yourself... you'll be surprised how many times you can find the answers here'), which gives the arc a sense of emotional completion and wisdom-transmission that the books do not contain. In the books, the ending is defined by loss and Harry's grief at what might have been. The film softens this into something closer to a meaningful goodbye.
character_arc.difference: The film's arc ends with Sirius delivering composed wisdom and Harry watching him fly away with a sense of bittersweet completion. The book's arc ends with Harry reading Sirius's letter on the train, feeling the weight of 'the home he might have had, and the fact that it was now impossible.' The film removes the letter, the Hogsmeade permission note, and the train scene - all of which are the arc's true emotional endpoint. The arc's conclusion is changed from loss to a kind of hopeful farewell.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc's structural beats are preserved, but the emotional resolution is meaningfully altered. The book's arc ends in grief and loss; the film's ends in warmth and wisdom. This is not mere compression - it changes what the arc means. The loss of the letter and train scene removes the arc's actual conclusion. Score in the 8-10 range: arc is incomplete and missing important steps.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Three key relationships: (1) Harry - Sirius is Harry's godfather, the last living connection to James, and the potential home Harry has never had. The dynamic is tentative and asymmetric: Sirius loves Harry but barely knows him, and his offer of a home is awkward and uncertain. (2) Lupin - old friends, Marauders, with a history of trust and betrayal (Sirius didn't tell Lupin about the switch; Lupin believed Sirius was the spy). Their reunion is emotional but complicated. (3) Pettigrew - former friend turned betrayer; Sirius's relationship with Pettigrew is defined by contempt, rage, and a specific moral argument about cowardice vs. courage.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Harry relationship is present but altered - the film's Sirius is more composed and mentor-like, and the offer of a home is less tentative. The Lupin relationship is present: the embrace, the 'old friend' dynamic, and the shared history are intact. The Pettigrew confrontation preserves the moral core. However, the full history of the Marauders - how they became Animagi for Lupin, the years of friendship, the specific texture of what Sirius lost - is compressed into brief references rather than the extended, emotionally rich account in the books.
key_relationships.difference: The Lupin-Sirius relationship loses the specific complication of mutual betrayal of trust (Sirius didn't tell Lupin about the switch; Lupin believed Sirius was guilty for twelve years). In the film, this is present but compressed. The Harry-Sirius relationship loses the awkward tentativeness of the book - the film's Sirius is more assured and mentor-like. The Marauder backstory, which gives all three relationships their depth, is significantly compressed.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The relationships are recognizable but the emotional dynamics are simplified. The Lupin-Sirius complication (mutual betrayal of trust) is present but not developed. The Harry-Sirius dynamic is altered by the addition of the sage-mentor register. Score in the 7-8 range: mostly faithful with some compression and simplification.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Book Sirius is complex in ways that resist simple summary: he is innocent but not entirely blameless (he suggested the switch to Pettigrew); he is loving but also capable of petty vindictiveness (bumping Snape's head in the tunnel); he is brave but also reckless to the point of endangering others (the Snape prank that nearly killed Snape); he is a victim of Azkaban but also someone who, by his own admission, was 'as good as' responsible for his best friends' deaths. He is also the source of the Marauder's Map, the Firebolt, and the Hogsmeade permission - practical acts of care that demonstrate his character across the year. The Three Broomsticks scene (heard by Harry) establishes his full history: best man at James's wedding, godfather, Secret-Keeper, betrayer - all before he appears in person.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film removes: the guilt about suggesting the switch to Pettigrew; the petty vindictiveness toward Snape in the tunnel; the Firebolt gift and its subplot; the Hogsmeade permission letter; the Three Broomsticks backstory scene (which in the books is Harry's first real understanding of who Sirius was); the Azkaban escape explanation (how he maintained sanity as a dog); the Crookshanks collaboration; the train letter. What remains is the confrontation, the Pettigrew exposure, the godfather offer, and the farewell. The film adds the invented sage-wisdom farewell.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The removed material is not decorative. The Three Broomsticks scene establishes Sirius as a fully realized person before Harry meets him - best man, godfather, the man who laughed at James's wedding. The Azkaban escape explanation is a key moment of self-disclosure that reveals how he survived (clinging to the knowledge of his innocence, not happiness). The Firebolt and letter show his practical care across the year. The guilt about the switch is the source of his tragic complexity. Without these, Sirius is reduced to: wronged man, loyal friend, potential godfather.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The removed material substantially reduces Sirius's complexity. He loses his guilt, his vindictiveness, his full backstory, his practical agency across the year, and his self-disclosure about Azkaban. The film adds a false sage-wisdom register that replaces complexity with sentiment. This is a significant flattening. Score in the 8-10 range: noticeable simplification with important nuances missing.

lost_or_transferred_material: Sirius's guilt about suggesting the switch from himself to Pettigrew as Secret-Keeper ('I as good as killed them')
The Firebolt gift and its subplot (Sirius sending it via Crookshanks from Azkaban)
The Hogsmeade permission letter at the end of the year
The Three Broomsticks scene establishing Sirius's full history before Harry meets him
Sirius's explanation of how he survived Azkaban (transforming into a dog, clinging to innocence rather than happiness)
The Crookshanks collaboration (Sirius communicating with the cat to steal passwords)
Sirius's petty vindictiveness toward Snape in the tunnel (making no effort to prevent Snape's head bumping the ceiling)
The train letter scene - the arc's true emotional endpoint establishing the loss of the home Harry might have had
The extended Marauder backstory told by Lupin and Sirius together in the Shrieking Shack
Sirius being physically restrained multiple times by Lupin from killing Pettigrew (his impulsive violence is reduced)
The invented sage-wisdom farewell ('Trust yourself... find the answers here') - added material with no book basis
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function, decision, or act of agency is transferred or removed): Sirius's guilt motivation, Firebolt agency, and Azkaban escape self-disclosure are removed. Cap applied, score held below 75.
Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove major complexity, agency, or contradiction): Multiple complexity elements removed. Cap confirmed not breached.
key_observations: The film's Sirius Black is recognizable and preserves the structural core of his role - wronged man, Pettigrew's pursuer, Harry's godfather - but loses the material that made him genuinely complex: his guilt about suggesting the switch to Pettigrew, his practical agency across the year (Firebolt, Crookshanks, letter), his self-disclosure about surviving Azkaban, and the full Marauder backstory. More damagingly, the film adds an invented sage-mentor register in the farewell scene that is tonally inconsistent with the book character and replaces the arc's true emotional endpoint (loss, grief, the destroyed possibility of family) with composed wisdom and bittersweet hope. The result is a Sirius who is simpler, warmer, and less tragic than the books establish.
